preFix.title: Fitting acoustic wall panels on timber battens
preFix.subtitle: Add sound insulation to a party wall using a batten and quilt system
preFix.excerpt: How to build an independent acoustic lining on a party wall using timber battens, mineral-wool sound-deadening quilt, and plasterboard, reducing airborne noise transmission without major construction work.
preFix.sourceNotes: Building Regulations Approved Document E (resistance to sound). BS 8233: guidance on sound insulation and noise reduction for buildings. Cassell's Cyclopaedia of Mechanics (partition construction). City and Guilds Level 2 Drylining.

content.text: An acoustic lining on a
party wall
works by adding mass and, more importantly, isolation between the two wall faces. A single plasterboard layer on battens with
acoustic quilt
in the cavity can reduce
airborne noise

by 10 to 15 decibels from the existing wall performance. The system fits entirely within your own property; it does not touch the party wall itself, so no Party Wall Act notice is required. The trade-off is floor space: a 75 mm batten system reduces the room width by 75 mm plus the plasterboard thickness.
What you will need: materials

Sawn or regularised timber battens, 75 x 50 mm (or 50 x 50 mm if space is more critical than performance). Acoustic mineral-wool quilt, 75 mm thick, density 45 to 60 kg/m3 (labelled as acoustic slab or acoustic quilt, not standard loft roll). Two layers of 12.5 mm plasterboard for the finished face, or one layer of 15 mm acoustic-grade plasterboard (denser than standard). Resilient bars (metal channels that decouple the plasterboard from the battens) add significant performance gain if budget allows. Jointing compound and 50 mm scrim tape. Acoustic sealant for sealing the perimeter of the lining to the floor, ceiling, and side walls.
Step 1, check the wall for services before fixing battens

Sweep a cable-and-pipe detector along the party wall in a grid pattern before drilling any fixing holes. Mark any hits with pencil. Party walls often carry cables from the neighbouring property as well as your own; the detector picks up cables on both sides of the wall if they are close enough. Mark a 50 mm exclusion zone around every hit. Drill the batten fixings 35 mm into the masonry minimum; use the SDS drill with a 6 or 7 mm masonry bit and plug-and-screw fixings.
Step 2, fix the floor and ceiling battens

Fix a horizontal batten along the floor and a horizontal batten along the ceiling, set back from the party wall by the depth of the vertical battens (typically 75 mm). Check them level with the spirit level. These sole and head plates locate the vertical battens and define the plane of the finished wall. Fix into the floor (or floor joist) and the ceiling joist above. Do not fix to the party wall itself: any contact between the new lining and the party wall creates a path for sound to bypass the air gap.
Step 3, fit the vertical battens at 600 mm centres

Cut vertical battens to fit between the sole and head plates. Fix them at 600 mm centres to align with the plasterboard sheet edges. The battens should not touch the party wall: aim for a 10 to 25 mm air gap between the batten back and the masonry. This gap allows the acoustic quilt to be stuffed in and reduces solid contact between the lining and the party wall. Check each vertical batten for plumb before fixing.
Step 4, fill the cavity with acoustic quilt

Cut the acoustic quilt into strips with the utility knife and push it into the cavity between the battens and the party wall. The quilt should fill the space without being compressed. Compression reduces its sound-absorbing performance. Wear protective gloves and a dust mask when handling mineral-wool quilt. Secure the quilt with light staples or by friction between the battens. Fill the full height of every bay from floor to ceiling.
Step 5, board the wall

Fix the first plasterboard layer to the battens using 32 mm plasterboard screws at 200 mm centres into each batten. Stagger the board joints so vertical joints in one layer do not coincide with joints in the second layer. If using resilient bars, fix the bars to the battens first and screw the plasterboard to the bars rather than directly to the timber. This isolates the board face from the batten and substantially improves performance. Tape and fill the joints in the usual way once all boards are fixed.
Step 6, seal the perimeter

Apply acoustic sealant around the entire perimeter of the lining where it meets the floor, ceiling, and side walls. Sound travels through small gaps far more readily than through solid surfaces; a gap of 1 mm around the edge of a well-constructed lining can halve its effective performance. Apply the sealant in a continuous bead and tool it into the joint. The skirting board and cornice will cover the sealant line once fitted.
Variations

For the best result in a bedroom or music room: two layers of 15 mm acoustic plasterboard on resilient bars with 100 mm quilt in the cavity. This can reach 40 dB of airborne-sound reduction. The room narrows by about 130 mm per treated wall. A quicker but weaker option is to glue two standard plasterboard sheets together and fix them directly to the party wall with dot-and-dab. This adds mass without a decoupled air gap and is faster but less effective.
Care

The acoustic lining needs no maintenance once decorated. Keep socket penetrations on the party wall face to a minimum: each back box opening reduces performance. Fill any gap around a back box with acoustic putty pads. Do not run pipes or cables through the cavity: any solid contact between the board face and the party wall bridges the air gap.

attrs.fix: Identify the flanking paths by pressing an ear to the floor, ceiling, and side walls while noise is coming from the neighbouring property. Treat the loudest path first. Skirting boards and cornices that span continuously from the new lining to the untreated floor or ceiling are common flanking paths.
attrs.cause: Flanking paths bypassing the lining: noise is travelling via the floor, ceiling, or side walls rather than through the party wall itself.
attrs.symptom: Little or no improvement in noise after the lining is built

attrs.fix: Drive screws to the point where the head is just flush with the paper face, with a slight dimple. A countersink that breaks through the paper loses most of its holding strength. Add a second screw 50 mm away.
attrs.cause: Screw driven too deep, breaking through the paper face and losing grip in the board.
attrs.symptom: Plasterboard screws pull through the board face when tightened

attrs.fix: Pack the gaps with folded card or thin timber strips before fixing. The batten must be fully supported at both ends; an unsupported end deflects under screwing pressure and the board will not lie flat.
attrs.cause: Out-of-level floor or ceiling causing gaps at the batten ends.
attrs.symptom: Battens not making full contact with the sole plate

initialFindings.message: Tutorial PUBLISHED with heroMediaId IS NULL — hero-fill must be run
PATTERN tutorial has no Method/Steps section and no orderedList — sequential instructions missing
tricolon: tricolon (three parallel items) — two adjectives almost always beats three
